coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/TiO2 nanocomposites for symmetric supercapacitors

coredata.dc:creator: Hoan, Tran Quoc
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) combined with TiO2 nanoparticles are a high performance electrode material for symmetric supercapacitors. The incorporation of NCDs significantly increases the specific capacitance compared to pure TiO2. The NCD-TiO2 anatase exhibits a specific capacitance of 390 F/g, at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The supercapacitor electrode based on anatase-doped NCD-TiO2 demonstrates high stability after 1000 charge-discharge cycles, the specific capacitance remains 92.6 % of the initial value, with an energy density of 13.2 Wh/kg and a power density of 250 W/kg.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
Carbon dots
TiO2 nanoparticles
Nitrogen doping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern, (b) FT-IR spectrum, (c) TEM images, (d) Size distribution histogram based on counting 50 nanoparticles of NCDs.

(a) CV curves of TiO2, NCDs, NCD-TiO2 anatase and NCD-TiO2 rutile at a scan rate of 10 mV/s, (b) different scan rates of NCD-TiO2 anatase, (c) GCD of NCD-TiO2 anatase, (d) specific capacitance of NCD-TiO2 anatase at different current densities.
(a) EIS, (b) EIS zoom plot, (c) GCD plot, and (d) GCD cyclic specific capacitance of NCD-TiO2 anatase.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical properties of nitrogen-doped carbon dots/TiO2 nanocomposites for symmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electric Power University, No. 235 Hoang Quoc Viet Street, Hanoi City, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electric Power University, No. 235 Hoang Quoc Viet Street, Hanoi City, Viet Nam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) combined with TiO2 nanoparticles are a high performance electrode material for symmetric supercapacitors. The incorporation of NCDs significantly increases the specific capacitance compared to pure TiO2. The NCD-TiO2 anatase exhibits a specific capacitance of 390 F/g, at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The supercapacitor electrode based on anatase-doped NCD-TiO2 demonstrates high stability after 1000 charge-discharge cycles, the specific capacitance remains 92.6 % of the initial value, with an energy density of 13.2 Wh/kg and a power density of 250 W/kg.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitor-based energy storage has garnered significant attention from the scientific community. Supercapacitors have the ability to provide energy quickly, have a long life, and particularly exhibit a higher charge–discharge rate than electrochemical batteries [1]. This makes supercapacitors an ideal solution for energy storage systems for hybrid electric vehicles, hand drills, backup power supplies for computers, and in renewable energy technology [2]. However, supercapacitors have low energy density, typically about 3–10 times smaller than electrochemical batteries [3]. There are compounds oxides used as electrodes for hybrid capacitors such as MnO2 [4], TiO2 [5], CoP [6], Fe3O4 [7], RuO2 [8]. Among them, RuO2 exhibits the highest specific capacitance and outstanding electrochemical properties. However, Ruthenium is a very expensive metal, making RuO2-based supercapacitors commercially unfeasible.

Titanium oxide is an alternative because TiO2 is abundant, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly. However, TiO2 has low specific capacitance due to slow ion diffusion and low electrical conductivity. Selvakumar et al synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles via a microwave method, achieving a specific capacitance of 122 F/g and stability up to 5000 cycles [9]. Xie et al synthesized polypyrrole/titania hybrid nanotubes combined with carbon dots (CDs), supercapacitors with specific capacitance up to 849 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g [10]. Lal, Mamta Sham, et al. hydrothermally synthesized TiO2 nanoparticles combined with single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) achieving a specific capacitance of 144 F/g, a specific energy of 20 Wh/kg, and a capacitance retention of 95 % after 50,000 cycles [11]. In this study, electrodes based on TiO2 materials combined with NCDs were fabricated by hydrothermal method. The electrochemical properties of the electrodes were also studied.

NCD was synthesized as described in [12], NCD was freeze-dried to obtain brown powder, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was dissolved in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone solvent with a concentration of 1 % (w/w) to prepare a binder solution. NCD powder was mixed with TiO2 powder and ground together with the binder solution. The slurry was coated on copper foil by the scraper method with a gap of 200 µm, dried at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain a working electrode (50 mg/cm2). The characterization methods are provided in the Supplementary Material (SM).

Fig. 1 (a) shows the XRD pattern of NCDs, the XRD pattern does not have sharp peaks but broad peaks with obtuse angles, indicating the amorphous nature of NCDs. The characteristic diffraction peak of NCDs appears at a 2θ angle of 24.5°, close to the (002) phase plane of graphite, at 27° [13].

The FT-IR spectrum of NCDs is shown in Fig. 1 (b), the intense peaks at ∼ 3416 cm−1 can be assigned to the –O–H stretching vibrations due to the H2O molecules remaining in the sample. The doublet peak at ∼ 2925 cm−1 represents the stretching of the C–H bond. The stretching vibrations in the conjugated structures C=O and C–O are at ∼ 1670 cm−1, respectively [14]. Furthermore, the band at 1012 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of C–N bond, the broad band at ∼ 700 cm−1 is assigned to N–H stretching [15].

Fig. 1 (c) shows that the NCDs are well dispersed and nearly spherical, with particle diameters ranging from 2 to 25 nm, The size distribution histogram in Fig. 1(d) indicates that the majority of particles have a diameter of approximately 12 nm.

Fig. 2 (a) shows that the cyclic voltammetry (CV) spectrum of TiO2 has a shape of curves without clear peaks but is almost rectangular, indicating that the energy storage mechanism of the capacitor is a charge storage mechanism based on a double layer capacitance. Upon adding NCDs to TiO2, weak redox peaks appear. These redox peaks may be due to the NCDs having functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl involved [16].

The specific capacitance value was calculated using formula (2) in Supplementary Material. For NCD-TiO2 anatase, it was estimated to be 392 F/g, while the value was 240 F/g for NCD-TiO2 rutile, 167 F/g for TiO2 anatase, and 135 F/g for NCDs. The specific capacitance was enhanced when NCDs were combined with TiO2. The rutile TiO2 has fewer oxygen defects, which slows down the oxidation–reduction reaction rate, resulting in a smaller specific capacitance value [17]. Fig. 2 (b) shows that the shape of the curve is almost unchanged when the scan rate is increased, which indicates that the material is highly stable. The specific capacitance of NCD-TiO2 gradually decreased with increasing scan rate, which is due to the relatively slow kinetic rate of electrochemical reactions at high scan rates and many sites that cannot be fully accessed [18].

Comparison of galvanostatic charge discharge (GCD) at current densities from 1 A/g to 12 A/g is shown in Fig. 2 (c) and Fig. 2 (d). The specific capacitance calculated by formula (3) in Supplementary Material is 380 F/g, 340 F/g, 320 F/g, 304 F/g, 270 F/g, 228 F/g, current densities of 1 A/g, 2 A/g, 4 A/g, 8 A/g, 12 A/g, respectively. The specific power and power density calculated by formula (4) in Supplementary Material, the highest values are 13.2 Wh/kg and 250 W/kg.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were recorded over the frequency range of 0.01 to 100 kHz. Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the Nyquist plot of the electrode. Fig. 3 (b) shows the calculated Rct values for 10 Ω, 6.7 Ω, 6.2 Ω, and 6 Ω electrodes, corresponding to TiO2 rutile, TiO2 anatase, NCD, NCD-TiO2 rutile, NCD-TiO2 anatase. Interestingly, the TiO2 rutile electrode has the highest internal charge transfer resistance compared to the other samples. The smallest Rct value of NCD-TiO2 anatase (6 Ω) may be due to NCD as a conductive agent, on the surface of NCDs there are functional groups that can undergo faradaic process to enhance the conductivity of the electrode, this process can affect the electrochemical performance of the capacitor such as power density [19].

Fig. 3 (c) is the GCD of NCD-TiO2 anatase after 500 and 1000 charge–discharge cycles, Fig. 3 (d) shows that the capacity of the supercapacitor electrode based on NCD-TiO2 anatase decreased from 380 F/g to 352 F/g after 1000 charge–discharge cycles, which is still 92.6 % of the original, indicating the stability of the material.

In this work, hydrothermal synthesis of NCDs combined with TiO2 nanoparticles as a high-performance electrode material for symmetric supercapacitors was performed. The synergistic effect of NCDs and TiO2 showed an enhancement of the specific capacitance and energy density of the supercapacitor. The maximum specific capacitance of the supercapacitor based on anatase NCD-TiO2 was achieved at 392 F/g and 352 F/g after 1000 cycles. The highest specific energy density was 13.2 Wh/kg corresponding to a specific power of 250 W/kg. Therefore, anatase NCD-TiO2 is an effective material for symmetric supercapacitor electrode.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2025.138780.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
